text: Hello friends. This patient has left-sided eight centimeter endometrioma.
The video shows the left side of the ovary adjacent to a para-ovarian endometrioma.

The surgeon observes the left ovary with visible follicles and a large para-ovarian endometrioma adhered to the ovarian fossa. The right ovary is slightly adhered but lacks endometrioma. A puncture is made in the endometrioma using a harmonic vibrating jaw to facilitate aspiration and mobilization from the ovarian fossa.

The surgeon enlarges the opening of the para-ovarian endometrioma and inserts a suction device to remove the chocolate-colored material from the cyst, with the patient's head elevated to prevent spillage into the upper abdomen.
The surgeon identifies the cul-de-sac where endometrial chocolate material is deposited. Suction is used to remove the material, followed by repeated irrigation to clear the area.

In this type of endometrioma which is adhered with the cul-de-sac it is important that you should do the adhesiolysis and separate it from ovarian fossa but care should be taken that that should be avoided using the

The surgeon uses the blunt tip of the suction irrigation device to separate the ovarian cyst from the ovarian fossa, taking care to avoid the ureter in the area. The para-ovarian cyst is then excised in its entirety.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to excise the para-ovarian cyst, applying andromedial traction with the left hand. The cyst is successfully removed using an endo bag.
The surgeon separates adhesions from the left utero sacral ligament and frees the ovary adhered in the ovarian fossa.
The surgeon continues the excision of the cyst wall, carefully removing it from the surrounding tissue until it is completely excised.
The surgeon uses suction irrigation to clean the surgical area, removing hair, oozing, and chocolate-colored material from the cyst. Following excision, the area is further treated with fulguration.
The surgeon performs fulguration on the bed of the cyst to prevent recurrence, following the cleaning of chocolate-colored material from the cul-de-sac using suction irrigation.

The uterus is observed in an anti-verted position. A suction irrigation tip is used to rub over the endometriotic area, removing debris. Subsequently, a spatula is employed to cauterize the bed of the endometrial cyst, aiming to prevent recurrence.

The surgeon performs fulguration on the entire ovarian side of the para-ovarian cyst to ensure no endometriotic material remains. The ovarian fossa, where the ovary was previously adhered, is also fulgurated to prevent recurrence.
In the left ovarian fossa, the surgeon performs fulguration to burn any remaining endometriotic tissue, ensuring complete removal of adhered tissue from the ovary.

The surgeon performs fulguration using electrosurgery, maintaining a 0.5 millimeter distance from the tissue to create a spray effect. This technique minimizes depth of injury while covering a wider surface area, avoiding damage to deeper structures such as the ureter or nerves.

The surgeon uses a spatula to perform fulguration on areas affected by endometriosis, including the posterior wall of the uterus, cul-de-sac, and uterosacral ligament. Following fulguration, suction and irrigation are repeatedly applied to remove burnt tissue.
The surgeon performs final irrigation and suction to remove remaining blood and fulgurated tissue char, revealing a surgical field with no visible bleeding.
The surgical field is clear, with clean fluid and no visible oozing. The surgeon prepares to use an interseed to prevent adhesion formation in the area.
The surgeon positions the uterus in an antiverted orientation and places a 15x15 interseed into the cul-de-sac, posterior wall of the uterus, and ovarian fossa to prevent adhesion formation.
In this patient we have done tubal potency test as well that was patent. Both the tubes are patent.
The surgeon displays the interseed, a reoxygenated cellulose material, spreading it over the surgical area. The interseed appears visually similar to a mesh but has the consistency of tissue paper.

The surgeon lifts the ovary and carefully positions the interseed between the ovary and ovarian fossa, ensuring it does not cover the ovary. The interseed is spread to cover the complete posterior wall of the uterus and the anterior wall of the rectum, aiming to prevent adhesion.

The surgeon uses two-hand instruments, including a grasper and a Maryland, to carefully spread the interseed over the uterosacral ligament, ovary, and ovarian fossa. The ovary is lifted and positioned posterior to the interseed, ensuring the sides of the interseed are pushed into the ovarian fossa to prevent adhesion.
The surgeon spreads the interseed over the left side ovarian fossa, where fulguration has been performed. The interseed appears shredded in this area, indicating its fragility.
The interseed is fully spread over the surgical area, and the assistant begins to slowly drop the uterus, which is currently intubated, while removing the uterine manipulation device.

The interseed is observed dropping down and being manipulated to ensure it adheres nicely around the surgical area. The field appears completely dry with no visible bleeding or oozing. The surgeon prepares for tissue retrieval.
The surgeon uses a gloves endo bag for tissue retrieval, carefully placing the excised tissue into the bag for removal from the surgical site.

The surgeon places the removed cyst into the endo bag and uses a 10mm umbilical port to extract the bag. A semi-traumatic clasp is used to pull the edges of the glove sleeve, allowing the contents to be removed with an O'Ham forceps.
The surgeon prepares to close the dilated port site using a varus needle after the removal of the endo bag containing the cyst.

The surgeon pierces the varus needle through one side of the wound, excluding the skin, and pushes a loop suture through. A grasper pulls the real suture into the loop suture, allowing the loop to extract the real suture. The process resembles a cobbler's needle used for stitching leather. The knot is tied outside the wound.
This is very important to prevent the hernia of the ten millimeter port. So wound closure should be very tight and it should include everything except skin. So thank you very much.
This was just a simple case of endometrioma. Have a nice day. God bless you.
